You are a DIRTY-ROOM integrator for the tamarin-rs relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. Read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md (integration rules) and the relevant INTEGRATION_REPORT.md sections first.
RULES: adapters and extraction only — NEVER transplant ported logic into clean (headerless) files; clean vendored sources get mechanical fixes only. A clean file acquiring a GPL header from scripts/gen_license_headers.py is a provenance violation: stop and report. If a gap still blocks, KEEP the ported path + report precisely — never force a swap. Comments describe current code only. No git commits. Append a dated section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md; return a plain-text summary.
FINAL VALIDATION: cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-parser -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-prover -p tamarin-server; the wellformedness fixture suite; scripts/gen_license_headers.py then --check (expect 0 stale). Report the header-count delta and any author citations that disappeared.

TASK: complete the unit-G swap (derivation checks). The round-4 clean crate (vendored at crates/tamarin-theory/src/deriv_check_clean/, audited pass) is behaviorally complete; the prior integrator identified two remaining items, both dirty-room work:
1. ADAPTER input normalization: the clean candidate collector treats declared nullary function symbols as App(name,[]) but the parser AST leaves them as Var(name). Write a thin, behavior-neutral pre-normalization in the ADAPTER (rewrite Var(n) -> App(n,[]) for names in the theory's declared nullary function set) before feeding the clean check. Do not modify the clean crate.
2. Implement the clean crate's batched DerivabilitySolver (check_rule) over the ported probe-theory solver (prove_probe/synthesise_probe_theory — the solver stays ported and headered): one saturation per rule answering all its variables.
Then BUILD A CORPUS GATE before swapping: run the current (ported) pipeline and the reference oracle (the wellformedness cluster's oracle script drives the v1.13.0 binary; PATH needs /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin) over a corpus of example theories that exercise Message Derivation Checks — include theories with macros, let-blocks, multi-variable rules, nat/fresh/pub sorts — and byte-compare the MDC topic. Then route through deriv_check_clean (via the adapter) and require the SAME byte output on the whole gate corpus plus all existing captured tests. NOTE: the previous integrator relocated a byte-neutral show_lvar helper INTO deriv_check.rs — rehome it before deleting. When green, DELETE the ported crates/tamarin-theory/src/deriv_check.rs and route all call sites through the clean orchestration. If any corpus theory diverges, keep-and-report with the exact input and byte diff.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

3	description: "USER DIRECTION: review workflows should fix-as-they-find, not report-then-reapply with fresh agents"

9	
10	For future review/cleanup workflows on the tamarin Rust port (and generally): agents should FIX issues as they find them, in the same agent context, instead of producing a findings report that a second fresh agent later re-discovers and applies (2026-07-10, after review #2 ran report-only and every dimension then needed a separate apply campaign).
11	
12	**Why:** the apply agent re-does the finder's code comprehension from scratch — duplicated effort, and the finding text is a lossy interface (line drift, prose-shaped suggestions, mislabeled files all showed up in practice).
13	
14	**How to apply:**
15	- Finder agents get edit rights: file-disjoint scheduling in the main tree (the elegance-wave pattern) or worktrees with patches applied in collision-aware order (the perf-batch pattern).
16	- Adversarial verification reviews the actual DIFF (concrete, cheap) instead of a finding description; a rejected diff gets reverted by the verifier or orchestrator.
17	- The safety rails stay: byte gate (subset per batch, full 402 at end), unit tests, and for perf the measure-then-keep bench discipline — but find→implement stays in ONE context; only keep/revert is external.
18	- Reports still get produced, but as a record of what was DONE (with bench/gate evidence), not as a to-do list.
19	
20	Canonical script (latest = round-8 rerun, 2026-07-15): `/home/kamilner/.claude/projects/-home-kamilner-tamarin-prover/72c6f916-c076-4166-9e6c-4fb19188a69c/workflows/scripts/quality-pass-r8-wf_e7ce5c34-be3.js` (round-3 original survives in the 3ca02e36 session's workflows/scripts/). The r8 prompt embeds the rounds-4-7 invariant list and the [CHANGED-SINCE-LAST-PASS] depth-weighting — regenerate partitions + changed-list from git before each rerun; don't reuse them stale. Seats: partition/special agents = opus worktree fix-inline; diff-audit = fable over the APPLIED diff (33/33-style per-hunk verdicts). Trap: worktree agents cannot see untracked files — any "file X does not exist" claim from a worktree agent must be re-checked in the real tree.
21	
22	**Whole-codebase two-wave pattern (2026-07-16, commits 6b718463 + 7c248c1b):** when the user asks for a WHOLE-tree cleanup (not depth-weighted at recent changes), run Wave A = cross-file/cross-crate dedup (6 lens finders → fable judge producing file-disjoint clusters with per-cluster instructions → opus worktree implementers; script `dedup-wave-a-wf_ad5dcec8-668.js` in this session's workflows/scripts/) then Wave B = per-partition polish with the churn dial LOOSENED ("reads well" bar, not minimal-diff; script `scratchpad/quality_pass_waveb.js`, superseding the r8/r9 zero-churn preamble). Findings: partition passes structurally cannot see cross-partition duplication — the dedup wave found 40 real findings after 5 clean quality passes; and after dedup + 5 passes, even the loosened dial yielded only 33 edits tree-wide, so the tree is at target quality — further whole-tree passes are diminishing returns. The judge's KNOWN-GOOD-DUPLICATION list (drifted System free-var traversals, two SubtermStore step ports, parser dependency-free edit_distance, non_injective_fact_instances* HS-distinct mirrors, three formula layers) is embedded in both scripts' preambles — carry it into any future pass so agents don't re-litigate. Audit-seat prompts should force per-edit verification against the CODE and HS sources, tiered by risk (behavior-touching vs comment-only).
23	
24	**The hygiene commit-pair is MANDATORY for perf/feature tranches too, not just comment-heavy campaigns** (2026-07-15: I skipped it for the round-10 perf commits; user caught it and asked "were these reviewed sensibly for elegance/comments? I don't want a bunch of complexity"; the belated audit (6c69fef1) found real residue — a production-dead pub wrapper, a hand-rolled env_gate! duplicate, 15 lines of closure boilerplate, narration comments — net −9 lines. Agent-authored patches especially need it: both tranche-2 patches also landed clippy debt.)
25	
26	Related: [[rust-port-perf]] (gate/bench discipline, round ledger), [[agent-model-policy]].

11	
12	**Why:** process artifacts live in git history and session scratchpads; a comment that narrates them goes stale silently (round 6 shipped two comments still describing the pre-`SealedEqStore` state within hours of writing them). The invariant the audit established is the useful content — state it directly.
13	
14	**How to apply:** when landing agent-produced patches, grep the diff for `audit|design §|§[0-9]|critique|census|former|used to|retired|subsume|this pass|sweep` before committing; rewrite hits to the bare invariant ("keyed lookup only, never iterated" stands alone; drop the "AUDIT (…):" label). Forward-looking maintenance instructions ("if you relax this gate, re-establish the carve-out") are fine; runtime-mechanism phrases ("a prior zero-signal pass set the marker") are fine. Cleanup precedents: 4ad61d80 (round-6 additions), 897367f7 (rounds 1–4 equivalence comments — the rewrite pattern for "byte-identical to the former X" proofs is: state what a rebuild WOULD produce and why it equals the kept value). Whole-repo grep is clean as of 897367f7 except two deliberate keeps: runtime pre/post phrasing and upstream-HS commit references (oracle documentation). Related: [[review-workflows-fix-inline]].

396	Blockers:
397	1. Report STRUCTURE. The ported `format_deriv_report` returns ONE
398	   `WfError("Message Derivation Checks", msg)` whose `msg` bakes in the
399	   `underline_topic` header + intro + all rule blocks (joined `\n\n`) — the Rust
400	   wf-report renderer does NOT add per-topic headers. The clean
401	   `message_derivation_checks` returns MANY `WfError`s (one intro + one per rule,
402	   NO underline header — it expects the renderer to add it). Routing clean would
403	   drop the "Message Derivation Checks\n====" header and re-shape the report;
404	   matching the ported bytes needs a reassembly adapter, not a thin one.
405	2. Report ORDER. The clean sorts flagged variables by `(sort_rank[Fresh<Msg],
406	   name, idx)` (BEHAVIOR.md §6); the ported emits them in HS `LVar`-Ord
407	   `(idx, sort_ord[Pub<Fresh<Msg<Node<Nat], name)` (`collect_rule_free_vars`).
408	   These are a genuine algorithmic conflict that diverges on rules with >=2
409	   non-derivable variables; both sides claim capture-parity, so their fixtures do
410	   not discriminate — an unresolved byte-parity risk.
411	3. Candidate SET. The clean collects ALL variables (premises+actions+conclusions
412	   +let_block, no `Pub`/`Node`/nullary-function exclusion) as a solver superset;
413	   the ported `collect_rule_free_vars` excludes `Pub`/`Node`/`Suffix` and
414	   user-nullary names and omits the let-block (it substitutes it first). Routing
415	   clean changes which probes are issued.

417	   expands theory `macros:` (`applyMacroInProtoRule`) and the rule `let{}` block
418	   (`apply_let_block`) INSIDE the check. The clean has no such pass — a genuine
419	   gap; the adapter would have to keep that ported pre-expansion.
420	5. Solver GRANULARITY. The ported drives the solver batch-per-rule — one
421	   synthesised probe theory with N per-variable lemmas, `closeTheoryWithMaude` +
422	   source saturation ONCE, reused across the N lemmas (HS Prover.hs:260-279). The
423	   clean trait asks per-variable (`check(one var)`); a literal impl re-saturates
424	   per variable (perf regression), so a faithful route needs a per-rule
425	   memoizing adapter.

427	emit the single-WfError/baked-header shape and the HS LVar order (or the

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Regenerate the per-file GPL-permission headers on ported Rust sources.
3	
4	Scans crates/**/*.rs for citations of upstream tamarin-prover Haskell files
5	(`Foo.hs`, `path/Foo.hs:123-456`, or dotted module paths like
6	`Theory.Constraint.Solver.AnnotatedGoals`), resolves them against the
7	submodule tree, blames the cited files at the pinned submodule HEAD, and
8	writes a header naming the upstream authors (GitHub usernames where known)
9	whose permission the file's derived content awaits.  Files with no upstream
10	citations get no header; stale headers are stripped.  Idempotent.
11	
12	Usage:
13	  scripts/gen_license_headers.py             # regenerate headers in place
14	  scripts/gen_license_headers.py --check     # exit 1 if any header is stale
15	  scripts/gen_license_headers.py --refresh-identities   # re-query GitHub
16	                                             # (gh CLI) for unknown emails
17	
18	Identity resolution order: committed cache (scripts/header_identities.json)
19	-> username embedded in @users.noreply.github.com emails -> GitHub commits
20	API via `gh` (only with --refresh-identities) -> git author name verbatim.
21	"""
22	
23	import argparse
24	import collections
25	import json
26	import os
27	import re
28	import subprocess
29	import sys
30	
31	REPO = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
32	SUB = os.path.join(REPO, "tamarin-prover")
33	CRATES = os.path.join(REPO, "crates")
34	CACHE = os.path.join(REPO, "scripts", "header_identities.json")
35	UPSTREAM_REPO = "tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover"
36	
37	HEADER1 = "// Currently GPL 3.0 until granted permission by the following authors:"
38	SOURCES_LINE = "// Ported from upstream tamarin-prover sources:"
39	MIN_LINES = 10          # authors below this per-file line count fold into the tail note
40	WRAP = 72
41	
42	# Contributors whose permission is not required (the porting author).
43	SELF_EMAILS = {
44	    "kamilner@kamilner.ca", "kevin.milner@cs.ox.ac.uk",
45	    "github@kamilner.ca", "kevinmilner@improbable.io",
46	}
47	# Cited files that are not tamarin-prover sources (external libraries).
48	EXTERNAL = {"HughesPJ.hs", "Text/PrettyPrint/HughesPJ.hs"}
49	
50	FILE_PAT = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_/.]*\.hs")
51	MODULE_PAT = re.compile(
52	    r"\b(?:Theory|Term|Sapic|Accountability|Main|Web|Items|Text|Utils)"
53	    r"(?:\.[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*)+\b")
54	
55	CRATE_PREF = {
56	    "tamarin-parser": lambda h: "/Text/Parser" in h,
57	    "tamarin-sapic": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/sapic/"),
58	    "tamarin-term": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/term/"),
59	    "tamarin-accountability": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/accountability/"),
60	    "tamarin-server": lambda h: h.startswith("src/Web/"),
61	    "tamarin-theory": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/theory/") and "/Text/Parser" not in h,
62	    "tamarin-utils": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/utils/"),
63	    "tamarin-prover": lambda h: h.startswith("src/"),
64	}
65	
66	
67	def git(args, cwd=SUB):
68	    return subprocess.run(["git", *args], cwd=cwd, capture_output=True,
69	                          text=True, check=False).stdout
70	
71	
72	def hs_files():
73	    return git(["ls-files", "*.hs"]).split()
74	
75	
76	def rust_files():
77	    for dirpath, dirs, files in os.walk(CRATES):
78	        dirs[:] = [d for d in dirs if d != "target"]
79	        for f in files:
80	            if f.endswith(".rs"):
81	                yield os.path.join(dirpath, f)
82	
83	
84	def module_to_candidates(dotted, tree):
85	    """Resolve a dotted module path, dropping trailing segments (which may be
86	    function names) until a file matches."""
87	    parts = dotted.split(".")
88	    for end in range(len(parts), 0, -1):
89	        suffix = "/".join(parts[:end]) + ".hs"
90	        m = [h for h in tree if h == suffix or h.endswith("/" + suffix)]
91	        if m:
92	            return m
93	    return []
94	
95	
96	def resolve_citations(text, crate, tree, global_qualified):
97	    """Return (resolved paths, ambiguous candidate tuples)."""
98	    resolved, ambiguous = set(), []
99	    cites = set(FILE_PAT.findall(text))
100	    # Dotted module paths count as derivation citations only in an explicit
101	    # "port of" context — bare mentions in module-map docs are references,
102	    # not derivation (see the uncited-file audit: those files are independent).
103	    for line in text.splitlines():
104	        if re.search(r"\b[Pp]ort(?:ed)?\b.*\bof\b", line):
105	            for dotted in MODULE_PAT.findall(line):
106	                cites.add(dotted + ".hs")  # normalized below
107	    for c in cites:
108	        c = c.lstrip("./")
109	        if c in EXTERNAL or os.path.basename(c) in EXTERNAL:
110	            continue
111	        m = [h for h in tree if h == c or h.endswith("/" + c)]
112	        if not m and "." in c[:-3]:
113	            m = module_to_candidates(c[:-3], tree)
114	        if not m:
115	            parts = c[:-3].replace(".", "/").split("/")
116	            for i in range(1, len(parts)):
117	                mm = [h for h in tree if h.endswith("/" + "/".join(parts[i:]) + ".hs")]
118	                if len(mm) == 1:
119	                    m = mm
120	                    break
121	        if len(m) == 1:
122	            resolved.add(m[0])
123	            global_qualified.add(m[0])
124	        elif m:
125	            ambiguous.append(tuple(sorted(m)))
126	    return resolved, ambiguous
127	
128	
129	def disambiguate(resolved, ambiguous, crate, global_qualified):
130	    basenames = {os.path.basename(h) for h in resolved}
131	    for cands in ambiguous:
132	        if any(os.path.basename(c) in basenames and c in resolved for c in cands):
133	            continue  # covered by a qualified citation in the same file
134	        pref = [c for c in cands if CRATE_PREF.get(crate, lambda h: False)(c)]
135	        pool = pref if pref else list(cands)
136	        if len(pool) > 1:
137	            g = [c for c in pool if c in global_qualified]
138	            pool = g if g else pool
139	        resolved.update(pool)  # conservative: keep all remaining candidates
140	    return resolved
141	
142	
143	def load_identities():
144	    if os.path.exists(CACHE):
145	        return json.load(open(CACHE))
146	    return {}
147	
148	
149	def identify(email, name, identities, refresh):
150	    if email in identities:
151	        return identities[email]
152	    m = re.match(r"(?:\d+\+)?([^@]+)@users\.noreply\.github\.com", email)
153	    if m:
154	        identities[email] = m.group(1)
155	        return identities[email]
156	    if refresh:
157	        out = subprocess.run(
158	            ["gh", "api", f"/repos/{UPSTREAM_REPO}/commits?author={email}&per_page=1",
159	             "-q", ".[0].author.login"],
160	            capture_output=True, text=True)
161	        login = out.stdout.strip()
162	        if out.returncode == 0 and login and login != "null":
163	            identities[email] = login
164	            return login
165	    identities[email] = name  # fallback: git author name verbatim
166	    return name
167	
168	
169	def blame_authors(path, identities, refresh, names_by_email):
170	    cnt = collections.Counter()
171	    author = None
172	    for line in git(["blame", "--line-porcelain", "HEAD", "--", path]).splitlines():
173	        if line.startswith("author "):
174	            author = line[7:]
175	        elif line.startswith("author-mail "):
176	            email = line[12:].strip("<>")
177	            if email in SELF_EMAILS:
178	                continue
179	            names_by_email[email] = author
180	            cnt[identify(email, author, identities, refresh)] += 1
181	    return cnt
182	
183	
184	def merge_name_fallbacks(identities, names_by_email, blame_cache):
185	    """An author with several emails may resolve to a username on one email
186	    and fall back to their git name on another.  Collapse: if a fallback
187	    label equals the git author name behind a username-resolved email, remap
188	    it to that username."""
189	    name_to_user = {}
190	    for email, ident in identities.items():
191	        if "@" not in ident and email in names_by_email:
192	            if ident != names_by_email[email]:  # ident is a username
193	                name_to_user[names_by_email[email]] = ident
194	            m = re.match(r"(?:\d+\+)?([^@]+)@users\.noreply\.github\.com", email)
195	            if m:
196	                name_to_user.setdefault(names_by_email[email], ident)
197	    remap = {n: u for n, u in name_to_user.items() if n in
198	             {i for i in identities.values()}}
199	    if not remap:
200	        return
201	    for email, ident in list(identities.items()):
202	        if ident in remap:
203	            identities[email] = remap[ident]
204	    for h, cnt in blame_cache.items():
205	        for name, user in remap.items():
206	            if name in cnt:
207	                cnt[user] += cnt.pop(name)
208	
209	
210	def wrap_comment(text):
211	    words, lines, cur = text.split(" "), [], "//  "
212	    for w in words:
213	        if len(cur) + len(w) + 1 > WRAP and cur.strip("/ "):
214	            lines.append(cur)
215	            cur = "//  "
216	        cur += " " + w
217	    lines.append(cur)
218	    return lines
219	
220	
221	def build_header(authors, sources):
222	    ranked = sorted(authors.items(), key=lambda kv: (-kv[1], kv[0]))
223	    major = [a for a, n in ranked if n >= MIN_LINES]
224	    minor = any(n < MIN_LINES for _, n in authors.items())
225	    names = ", ".join(major)
226	    if minor:
227	        names += ", and other minor contributors (see upstream git history)"
228	    out = [HEADER1, *wrap_comment(names), SOURCES_LINE,
229	           *wrap_comment(", ".join(sorted(sources)))]
230	    return "\n".join(out) + "\n\n"
231	
232	
233	def strip_header(src):
234	    if src.startswith(HEADER1):
235	        return src.split("\n\n", 1)[1] if "\n\n" in src else ""
236	    return src
237	
238	
239	def main():
240	    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
241	    ap.add_argument("--check", action="store_true")
242	    ap.add_argument("--refresh-identities", action="store_true")
243	    args = ap.parse_args()
244	
245	    tree = hs_files()
246	    identities = load_identities()
247	    global_qualified = set()
248	    per_file = {}
249	
250	    for rs in rust_files():
251	        body = strip_header(open(rs, encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").read())
252	        crate = os.path.relpath(rs, CRATES).split(os.sep)[0]
253	        resolved, ambiguous = resolve_citations(body, crate, tree, global_qualified)
254	        per_file[rs] = (body, crate, resolved, ambiguous)
255	
256	    blame_cache, names_by_email = {}, {}
257	    file_sources = {}
258	    for rs, (body, crate, resolved, ambiguous) in per_file.items():
259	        sources = disambiguate(set(resolved), ambiguous, crate, global_qualified)
260	        file_sources[rs] = sources
261	        for h in sources:
262	            if h not in blame_cache:
263	                blame_cache[h] = blame_authors(h, identities,
264	                                               args.refresh_identities,
265	                                               names_by_email)
266	    merge_name_fallbacks(identities, names_by_email, blame_cache)
267	
268	    changed, stale = 0, []
269	    for rs, (body, crate, resolved, ambiguous) in sorted(per_file.items()):
270	        sources = file_sources[rs]
271	        agg = collections.Counter()
272	        for h in sorted(sources):
273	            agg.update(blame_cache[h])
274	        new = (build_header(agg, sources) + body) if sources and agg else body
275	        cur = open(rs, encoding="utf-8", errors="replace").read()
276	        if new != cur:
277	            stale.append(os.path.relpath(rs, REPO))
278	            if not args.check:
279	                open(rs, "w", encoding="utf-8").write(new)
280	                changed += 1
281	
282	    json.dump(identities, open(CACHE, "w"), indent=1, sort_keys=True)
283	    if args.check:
284	        for f in stale:
285	            print(f"STALE: {f}")
286	        print(f"{len(stale)} stale header(s)")
287	        sys.exit(1 if stale else 0)
288	    print(f"updated {changed} file(s); identities cached: {len(identities)}")
289	
290	
291	if __name__ == "__main__":
292	    main()
293

Now I'll build the full corpus gate with the required feature coverage (macros, let-blocks, multi-var, nat/fresh/pub via both prefix and suffix), capture oracle ground truth, then implement the swap. Let me create the corpus.

# 2. multi-variable, two rules (reSign-style + hash) — order + multi-block
cat > $C/mdc_multivar.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCmultivar begin

functions: h/1, sign/3, extract/1

rule reSign:
  [ In(<sk1, r1>), In(sign(m, r2, sk2)) ]
  --[ Re(m) ]->
  [ Out(sign(m, r1, sk1)) ]

rule Second:
  [ In(h(a)), In(h(b)) ]
  --[ Ev(a, b) ]->
  [ Out('done') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Re('x') @ i | Ex #j. Ev('a','b') @ j"

end
EOF

# 3. macros — theory-level macro that wraps a var in a one-way fn
cat > $C/mdc_macro.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCmacro begin

functions: h/1
macros: wrap(z) = h(z)

rule R:
  [ In(wrap(w)) ]
  --[ Ev(w) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Ev('w') @ i"

end
EOF

# 4. let-block — let-bound var expands to inner non-derivable var
cat > $C/mdc_let.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDClet begin

functions: h/1

rule R:
  let y = h(w)
  in
  [ In(y) ]
  --[ Ev(w) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Ev('w') @ i"

end
EOF

# 5. nat sort via prefix %n, fresh ~x, mixed with derivable msg
cat > $C/mdc_sorts_prefix.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCsortsprefix begin

builtins: natural-numbers
functions: h/1

rule R:
  [ In(h(<~x, %n>)) ]
  --[ Ev(~x, %n) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i x n. Ev(x, n) @ i"

end
EOF

# 6. nat/fresh/pub via SUFFIX form
cat > $C/mdc_sorts_suffix.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCsortssuffix begin

builtins: natural-numbers
functions: h/1

rule R:
  [ In(h(<a:fresh, b:nat, c:msg>)) ]
  --[ Ev(a, b, c) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Ev(~'a', %1, 'c') @ i"

end
EOF

# 7. pub var present (must be derivable) alongside non-derivable msg
cat > $C/mdc_pub.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCpub begin

functions: h/1

rule R:
  [ In(h(m)), In($p) ]
  --[ Ev(m, $p) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Ev('m', $x) @ i"

end
EOF

# 8. no_derivcheck attribute — rule skipped
cat > $C/mdc_nodcheck.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCnodcheck begin

functions: h/1

rule Skipped [no_derivcheck]:
  [ In(h(w)) ]
  --[ Ev(w) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

rule Checked:
  [ In(h(q)) ]
  --[ Ev2(q) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Ev('w') @ i | Ex #j. Ev2('q') @ j"

end
EOF

# 9. nullary function constant — must NOT be flagged as a var
cat > $C/mdc_nullary.spthy <<'EOF'
theory MDCnullary begin

functions: h/1, c/0

rule R:
  [ In(h(<c, w>)) ]
  --[ Ev(w) ]->
  [ Out('ok') ]

lemma trivial: exists-trace "Ex #i. Ev('w') @ i"

end
EOF

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Corpus gate: for each theory, compare the MDC topic block of RS vs the oracle.
# Usage: gate.sh <rs-binary> <file1.spthy> [file2.spthy ...]
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
export MAUDE_PATH=/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/maude
ORACLE="/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover"
SCRATCH=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
EX="$SCRATCH/extract_mdc.sh"
RS="$1"; shift
pass=0; fail=0; nomdc=0
for f in "$@"; do
  base=$(basename "$f" .spthy)
  o="$SCRATCH/out/${base}.oracle.mdc"
  r="$SCRATCH/out/${base}.rs.mdc"
  mkdir -p "$SCRATCH/out"
  # cache oracle output (slow); re-extract cheaply
  ocache="$SCRATCH/out/${base}.oracle.full"
  if [ ! -s "$ocache" ]; then
    timeout "${ORACLE_TIMEOUT:-120}" "$ORACLE" "$f" 2>/dev/null > "$ocache"
  fi
  bash "$EX" "$ocache" > "$o"
  timeout "${RS_TIMEOUT:-120}" "$RS" "$f" 2>/dev/null > "$SCRATCH/out/${base}.rs.full"
  bash "$EX" "$SCRATCH/out/${base}.rs.full" > "$r"
  if [ ! -s "$o" ] && [ ! -s "$r" ]; then
    echo "NO-MDC   $base"; nomdc=$((nomdc+1)); continue
  fi
  if diff -q "$o" "$r" >/dev/null; then
    echo "MATCH    $base"; pass=$((pass+1))
  else
    echo "DIFFER   $base"; fail=$((fail+1))
    echo "  --- oracle vs rs (unified) ---"
    diff <(cat -A "$o") <(cat -A "$r") | sed 's/^/  /'
  fi
done
echo "=== gate: $pass MATCH, $fail DIFFER, $nomdc NO-MDC ==="
[ "$fail" -eq 0 ]

## G.2 The two dirty-room items (both done in the adapter)

1. **Nullary-Var->App input normalization.** The parser leaves a bare identifier

   `Var(name)->App(name,[])` for every `name` in the theory's declared-nullary set

   `collect_all_nullary_fun_names`), on rule facts + let-blocks + macro bodies,
   before feeding the clean check. Behaviour-neutral; matches the ported
   name-based deny-list. Verified: `mdc_nullary` (a `c/0` constant is not flagged).

   The same normalization pass ALSO folds each variable's sort onto its class
   representative (`Suffix(X)->X`, `Untagged->Msg`) — the exact classes the ported
   `sort_ord` deduped/ordered by. This is required because the clean crate keys
   variable identity on the full `VarSpec` and its `render_variable`/`var_order_key`
   do not special-case `Suffix(_)`. Without it a `x:nat` renders `x` not `%x`, and
   a variable appearing as both bare `c` and `c:msg` lists twice. Verified by the
   discriminating `mdc_sorts_suffix` fixture (`In(h(<a:fresh,b:nat,c:msg>))` with a
   bare-`a,b,c` action): oracle `~a, a, b, c, %b` reproduced byte-for-byte
   (one `c`, index-primary then Fresh<Msg<Nat ordering). Macro bodies get the
   nullary rewrite but NOT the sort fold, so macro-argument substitution still
   matches by identity.

2. **Batched `DerivabilitySolver` over the ported solver.** `check_rule(&RuleProbe)`
   resolves public/timepoint candidates as trivially derivable without probing
   (they never belong in the synthesised `Fr(..)` premises, exactly as the ported
   `collect_rule_free_vars` pub/node exclusion), then synthesises ONE probe theory
   for the remaining candidates and calls `prove_probe` ONCE — a single saturation
   answering all of the rule's variables. Non-`Solved` (incl. deadline) maps to
   `NotDerivable`, reproducing the ported conservative timeout policy exactly (the
   clean `TimeoutPolicy` branch is therefore never taken); an elaboration failure
   (`None`) leaves the whole rule unreported, as the ported `continue` did.

## G.3 Corpus gate — and the latent ported bug it caught

Gate = the MDC topic block of RS vs the v1.13.0 oracle (`hs_oracle.sh` binary,
`/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin` on PATH), byte-compared with `cat -A`.
